question: What are the main components to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied?

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. It interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing continuous monitoring and control.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain proper water chemistry, and uphold fuel design limits during normal and off-normal conditions for each NuScale Power Module.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system components and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying types of steel and nickel-based alloys used for corrosion resistance and compatibility, all in accordance with relevant BPVC standards. It also references where further material specifications can be found within the report and lists one reference source.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspections and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest ASME BPVC Section XI requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of each NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, all designed to meet regulatory definitions and maintain pressure containment.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operation and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC-endorsed standards, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module's Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and emergency valves, and associated piping. It details the integration of these components within the RPV, outlines their boundaries between primary and secondary systems, and references where further design specifics can be found in other report sections.

edges.description: Section A describes the RCS as including pressure-retaining portions that maintain the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B specifies that the pressurizer, as part of the reactor vessel, is directly exposed to and communicates RCS pressure and temperature, thus identifying it as a main component to which RCS design pressure and temperature are applied.
Section B lists the main components of the RCS (such as the RPV and PZR) to which the design pressure and temperature described in Section A are applied.

Section A describes the pressure-retaining portions of the RCS that maintain the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B lists specific components (such as the PZR, RVVs, RRVs, and degasification line) that are subject to the RCS design pressure and temperature, thus specifying which components the design values apply to.

Section A describes the pressurizer as an integral component of the reactor vessel, while Section B lists the pressurizer and reactor pressure vessel as main components of the RCS to which design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section A describes the pressurizer as a main component of the RCS that is subject to design pressure and temperature, while Section B lists system components and operational steps involving the pressurizer, indicating that both sections identify the pressurizer as a key component to which RCS design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section A lists the main components of the RCS, such as the reactor pressure vessel and integral pressurizer, to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied, while Section B describes operational procedures involving these same components, confirming their role as primary recipients of RCS design conditions.

Section A lists specific pressurizer components that are part of the RCS, while Section B describes the pressure-retaining portions of the RCS, connecting them by identifying which components are subject to RCS design pressure and temperature.

Section A specifies the individual components and materials that make up the pressurizer, which Section B generally describes as the upper region of the reactor vessel and its separation from the RCS by a baffle plate.
Section A details specific components of the pressurizer, which is identified in Section B as one of the main components of the RCS to which design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section A details the materials and design standards for threaded fasteners used in key RCS components, which are among the pressure-retaining portions described in Section B as maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary to which design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section A describes threaded fasteners used in RCS pressure boundary components, which are subject to the RCS design pressure and temperature, while Section B describes the pressurizer as an integral RCS component that is also subject to these same design conditions, thus both sections identify main components to which RCS design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section B identifies the main components of the RCS (such as the RPV and PZR) to which design pressure and temperature are applied, while Section A discusses the materials and inspection requirements for fasteners used in these same components, thus connecting the design basis to specific component implementation.

Section A defines the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied, while Section B describes specific RCS components and their pressure-retaining functions that support the integrity of the RCPB.

Section A defines the reactor coolant pressure boundary components to which RCS design pressure and temperature apply, while Section B describes the pressurizer as an integral part of the reactor vessel and thus one of those components.

Section A defines the reactor coolant pressure boundary components to which design pressure and temperature apply, while Section B lists the main components of the reactor coolant system, thus specifying which physical parts are subject to those design conditions.

Section B identifies the pressure-retaining portions of the RCS as components to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied, while Section A references the applicable ASME BPVC codes that specify the design requirements for those components.

Section A discusses the application of ASME code requirements for the design and integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the pressurizer as a main component of the reactor coolant system that is subject to the RCS design pressure and temperature.

Section B identifies the main components of the RCS (such as the reactor pressure vessel and integral pressurizer) to which the design pressure and temperature, as governed by the overpressure protection requirements and codes referenced in Section A, are applied.

Section A lists the main components of the RCS, while Section B describes the pressure-retaining portions of the RCS that maintain the reactor coolant pressure boundary, thus both sections identify the components to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section A lists the pressurizer as one of the main components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) to which design pressure and temperature are applied, while Section B provides detailed information about the pressurizer's integration and function within the RCS.

Section A lists the specific components (e.g., RPV, pressurizer, steam generators) that are subject to the RCS design pressure and temperature, while Section B provides a general description of the RCS and identifies its main components, thus Section A provides detailed specifications for the general components described in Section B.

Section A lists specific pressurizer and RPV components that are part of the RCS and subject to design pressure and temperature, while Section B identifies threaded fasteners in these same components as being designed and selected to withstand those conditions.

Section A lists specific pressurizer components that are part of the RCS, while Section B defines the RCPB as including all pressure-retaining components of the RCS, thereby indicating that the components in Section A are among those to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied as described in Section B.

Section A identifies specific pressurizer components that are subject to RCS design pressure and temperature, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that these components must comply with for pressure boundary integrity.

Section A specifies the individual components and materials of the pressurizer, which is one of the main components listed in Section B as part of the RCS to which design pressure and temperature are applied.

Section A lists specific components (e.g., RPV main closure flange, PZR heater bundle closures, RCS piping flanges, RSV flanges) that use threaded fasteners designed to withstand RCS pressure and temperature, while Section B defines the RCPB as including the pressure-retaining components of the RCS, thus connecting the components in A as part of the boundary described in B.

Section A describes specific RCS components (e.g., RPV main closure flange, PZR heater bundle closures, RCS piping flanges, RSV flanges) that are subject to design requirements, while Section B references the applicable ASME BPVC codes that set the design pressure and temperature criteria for these components.

Section A discusses the materials and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners used in main RCS components, while Section B lists those main RCS components (e.g., RPV, PZR, SGs, RSVs) to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied, thus connecting the fastener requirements in A to the components identified in B.

Section A identifies the pressure-retaining components of the reactor coolant system (RCS) that must withstand the design pressure and temperature, while Section B discusses the codes and standards (ASME BPVC) used to ensure these components are properly designed, fabricated, and constructed to those requirements.
Section A defines the reactor coolant pressure boundary components to which design pressure and temperature apply, while Section B lists the main RCS components included within that boundary.

Section B lists the main components of the RCS to which design pressure and temperature are applied, while Section A discusses the overpressure protection and applicable codes governing their design, thus Section A provides the regulatory framework that Section B's components must satisfy.
